subject: Astronomy & Space
title: The Oort Cloud
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: The Oort Cloud.

hook.text: A shell of trillions of icy objects may surround the solar system up to 2 light-years out. Nobody has seen it.

buildup.text: Its existence is inferred from long-period comets that arrive from random directions with huge orbits.

discovery.text: Jan Oort proposed it in 1950 to explain these comets. The theory has held up for over 70 years.

twist.text: Passing stars can nudge Oort Cloud objects inward, sending comets plunging sunward after millions of years.

climax.text: The Oort Cloud marks the true boundary of the Sun's gravitational reach—the edge of our solar system.

punchline.text: Trillions of comets in the dark. The Sun's outermost realm.

quiz.question: Why do scientists believe the Oort Cloud exists even though no one has seen it?
options: Long-period comets arriving from all directions imply a spherical source far away
Telescopes have detected faint infrared glow from its icy bodies
Radio signals from the Voyager probes detected it directly
